text:
An Italian television commentator has complained that theOlympic 2024coffee in the press room looks like it has been made with water from the River Seine. Olympic chiefs have come under fire for failing to deal with high pollution levels in France’s second-longest river.

French authoritiesspent a whopping £1.2billion to clean up the River Seineahead of the Olympics.

But there is still sewage floating in it, with E. coli levels consistently falling short of minimum standards of competition throughout the Games.

The River Seine pollution levels have been a major talking point of the Olympics 2024, with triathlon training cancelled on Sunday ahead of the event.

And earlier this week, an Italian broadcaster alluded to the waterway as he commentated on a swimming event at the Paris Olympic Aquatic Centre.

The commentator, who was working for Italian chancel Rai 2, claimed “there is coffee that looks like pandan with the waters of the Seine”. And he added that the caffeine-rich drink was “probably made with the same substances, with the same water, then diluted with low quality soluble”.

Pick up your ultimate guide to the Olympics! Going for Gold provides the day-by-day rundown of what's on and when. Clickhereto buy.

Read more:Fred Siriex’s comments about Team GB star daughter Andrea speak volumes



Discovery+is the new home of the Olympics. The streamer is set to broadcast 3,800 hours of sport live from Paris.

Sports fans can getDiscovery+ for £3.99a month until the end of 2024 via Amazon Prime's video channels. This also comes with anAmazon Prime 30-day free trial.

As well as the concerns about E. coli, there have beenfears over athletes catching a disease related to rat urine, which can cause flu-like symptoms as well as liver and kidney damage in some cases.

And ahead of the triathlon, USA OlympianSeth Rider admitted he had taken extreme measuresto build up an immunity to E. coli by refusing to wash his hands after going to the toilet.

“We actually raced here last year in the test event,” the 27-year-old stated. “I don't think anyone got sick after that, which can't be said about all the races we do. In preparation for this race, I knew there was going to be some E. coli exposure.

Don't miss:Why aren't Jonny and Alistair Brownlee competing at the Paris Olympics?

Don't miss:Olympic triathlon athlete in nasty crash as police rush to help stricken star

Don't miss:Beth Potter wins Olympic medal for Team GB after thrilling triathlon battle

“So I've been trying to increase my E. coli threshold by exposing myself to a bit of E. coli in day-to-day life. It's a proven method. Backed by science. It's just little things, throughout your day. Like not washing your hands after you go to the bathroom and stuff like that.”

Swimming in the River Seine has been banned since 1923 because of boat traffic and filthy water. But from next year, there are plans for Parisians and visitors to be given permission to take a dip into certain swimming areas following the clean-up.

And last year, deputy Paris mayor in charge of the Olympics Pierre Rabadan said: “When people see athletes swimming in the Seine with no health problems, they'll be confident themselves to start going back in the Seine. It's our contribution for the future.”

title: TV commentator complains Olympic coffee is 'made with polluted River Seine water' | Other | Sport | Express.co.uk
author: Tom Parsons
description: Olympics 2024 events have been impacted by the poor water quality in the River Seine.
